titlestmt.titleproper: Pathways to Life Quality Study surveys23-27-3281

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2022-09-19 18:17:18 UTC.

ead.archdesc:
======= RMA03281Pathways to Life Quality Study surveys23-27-3281Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections2B Carl A. Kroch LibraryCornell UniversityIthaca, NY 14853rareref@cornell.eduURL: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2022-09-19 18:27:04 UTC.appm >>>>>>> 2e3ec5c57314077b8435567632a49d313357daab
English
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Pathways to Life Quality Study surveys
New York State College of Human Ecology. Pathways to Life Quality Study
Moen, Phyllis.
Krout, John A.
23-27-3281
65.7 cubic feet. 65.7 cubic feet.
1995-2000. 4754983
Restrictions on Access
The names and addresses of individuals cannot be disclosed in any way (writing, speaking, publishing, etc.) unless the individual gives express permission. All users of this collection must sign an agreement to abide by this restriction.
Scope and content
Includes surveys from 1995-2000, project reports, student materials and photographs.
Preferred Citation
Pathways to Life Quality Study surveys, #23/27/3281. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
Biographical / Historical
The Pathways to Life Quality Study is designed to promote understanding of patterns of housing choices and residential transitions over the life course. The Continuing Care Retirement Community (CCRC) Study started in the fall of 1995 when Professor John A. Krout of Ithaca College and Professor Phyllis Moen of Cornell University developed a survey to administer to individuals planning to move into this new community. In the summer of 1997 these people were surveyed again, about a year and a half after their move to the CCRC. In the fall of 1997 Krout and Moen developed a more elaborate research design and began collecting data from almost 800 more individuals ages 60 and over living in a variety of different housing arrangements.
Older people -- United States. Retirement, Places of -- United States -- Case studies. Older people -- Housing -- New York (State) -- Ithaca. Photographs. Continuing Care Retirement Community at Ithaca
